Description of support provided
Dr Duncan is a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian and Lecturer in Nutrition and Dietetics employed by
both Guys and Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust and King's College London . He completed an NIHR Clinical
Doctoral Research Fellowship i
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
n 2016 in Nutrition and Infectious Diseases with clinical research interests in health
inequalities, ageing, HIV care, obesity, diabetes, and qualitative research methodologies.
Dr Duncan has provided me with excellent support during my pre-doctoral fellowship and with developing this
application. He has agreed to provide formal mentorship on a bimonthly basis with informal meetings between as
required. Dr Duncan has been invaluable in recommending training courses, broadening my thinking of different
methodological approaches, and introducing me to research dietitians in his network.
Dr Duncan has successfully developed a role as a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian delivering on each of the
four pillars of advanced clinical practice (clinical practice, leadership, research and education). He has secured a
£10 million award from the Southeast London Integrated Care Board to fund clinical care for people living with
complex obesity and research addressing health inequalities these patients experience through his team of PhD
and MSc researchers. He is well-placed to support me with navigating my NIHR journey and accessing
opportunities towards my future career aspirations of bec
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
oming a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian. Dr
Duncan has a track record of successful mentorship and is able to support me through the challenges that I may
encounter during my DCAF and development as a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian.
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 2
Name Professor Peter Brennan
Institution Portsmouth University Hospitals NHS Trust
Consultant Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeon and Honorary Professor of
Position
Surgery
Description of support provided
Professor Brennan is a Consultant Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeon with clinical interests in HNC reconstructive
free-flap surgery and patient safety. He has an MD in cancer biology and a PhD in human factors. Professor
Brennan was awarded a personal chair in surgery in recognition of his research and education profile, has
supervised 6 PhDs and examined many MDs and PhDs. He has contributed to several surgical textbooks, lead
editor of Gray’s Surgical Anatomy and editor-in-chief of Journal of Oral Pathology and Medicine. Professor
Brennan is integrated internationally with positions that include: Elected Council Member of the Royal College of
Surgeons, British Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons and BAHNO counc
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
il.
My research proposal includes development of a post-operative feeding toolkit in patients with HNC. Surgeons will
be key users of this toolkit and it is vital that I have a surgeon involved in my research, for example, by advising
me on outcome measures for my cohort study pertaining to disease and surgical characteristics that influence
eating/drinking after surgery.
Professor Brennan is well placed to support me with my research given his clinical and research background and
interests. I have developed an excellent working relationship with him through working together on BAHNO council
for three years and co-authoring publications including national standards. He will provide ongoing support as a
surgical advisor on my research, dissemination and mentor by helping me develop my professional networks in
HNC which is crucial for conducting future multi-centre studies and implementing my research within surgical units.
Collaborations
Professor Olle Ljungqvist
Professor of Surgery at Orebro University Hospital, Sweden and an international expert in ERAS. He chaired the
European Society for Clinical Nutrition and the International Association for Surgical Metabolism and Nutrition.
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
Professor Ljungqvist co-founded the ERAS Society, has successfully led and implemented novel ERAS research
internationally and contributed to multiple ERAS consensus guidelines. Professor Ljungqvist has been advising me
and will provide ongoing support on the design of my research from an ERAS perspective to optimise future
implementation alongside supporting my professional development by organising an overseas placement in
Stockholm visiting two ERAS centres of excellence to develop the skills, knowledge and networks to achieve my
aspirations for ERAS at UCLH.
Clinical Associate Professor Merran Findlay
Executive Research Lead (Cancer Nutrition) at Prince Alfred Hospital and Chris O’Brien Lifehouse Cancer Centre
in Sydney, Australia. An Advanced Accredited Practising Dietitian, she holds two current fellowships as Research
Translation Fellow, University of Sydney and Senior Research Fellow, University of New South Wales. A/Prof
Findlay has expertise in nutritional assessment methods and has advised me on most appropriate outcome
measures for my cohort study. Furthermore, A/Prof Findlay has expertise in implementation science and
integrating evidenced-based nutrition care guideli
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
nes into practice, advising me on my research from an
implementation perspective and will provide ongoing support. A/Prof Findlay has led international HNC dietetic
guidelines, contributed to HNC ERAS consensus guidelines and implementation science networks in Australia and
will support me with building networks and implementing my findings.
Professor Gareth Ambler
Professor in Medical Statistics at UCL with research interests and expertise in regression analysis and developing
prediction models. Professor Ambler has been advising me on the statistical analysis plan for my cohort study and
has been costed into my application to provide ongoing support during my PhD.
Ms Ursula Mackie-Savage
HNC ERAS Nurse at UCLH and leads on implementing and auditing ERAS protocols. Ursula will support me with
implementing my findings locally.
British Association of Head and Neck Oncologists (BAHNO)
National HNC organisation and a gatekeeper for endorsing recommendations. BAHNO and council members in
major UK HNC units will assist with advertising my survey to their membership which includes key decision-
makers for post-operative feeding practices.
British Dietetic Association (BDA) HNC group
I si
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
t on the committee which has dietetic representatives from major UK HNC centres nationally and will act as key
links for gaining survey responses. This includes Mr Long Li, dietetic representative on NHS England Getting It
Right First Time (GIRFT) HNC workstream which aims to review UK HNC service delivery and identify/reduce
variation in care. Long will review my survey questions and support me with accessing HNC unit leads alongside
implementing my findings.
Nutricia Medical, Danone
International market leader in clinical nutrition products including specialist ERAS formulas utilised at UCLH and
major funder of clinical nutrition trials. I have 1:1 time agreed with Dr Rebecca Carpenter (UK Senior Clinical
Research Manager) to learn about industry partnerships where future research collaboration and funding could be
sought.
UCLH Centre for Nursing, Midwife and Allied Health research
A community who has supported me on my clinical academic journey through mock interviews, my pre-doctoral
fellowship, monthly learning sets and peer support.
Young Tongues
A global peer-to-peer support group for people with tongue cancer which is the most prevalent cancer sub-site in
my research. Young
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
Tongues have supported me with advertising for PPI members and will provide ongoing
support by advertising and disseminating my research to patient networks.
UCL HNC Academic Centre
Academic arm of UCLH HNC Centre with monthly seminars that reach wide international audiences. Will support
me with networking and disseminating my research.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support should be composed jointly by the Heads of Department / Senior Managers of
the employing host organisation and the partner organisation.
The Heads of Department / Senior Managers of these organisations will be able to add the statement of support to
the form once they have confirmed participation. Invitations to participate are sent by the applicant via the
'Participants and Signatories' section of the form.
Statement of support
NHS Host organisation
University College London Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust (UCLH) is a member of the Shelford Group which is a
collaboration of the ten largest teaching and research NHS hospital trusts in England. As a Trust featuring in the
top 10 for research activity, we are committed to embedding research capability and capacity into all health
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
professions, irrespective of grade. This is reflected in the UCLH Strategy for 2023-27, in which we commit to
fostering clinical academic careers within nursing, midwifery and allied health professions (NMAHP). We aim to
achieve the national target of 1% of NMAHP in clinical academic roles by 2030 through identifying NMAHPs who
have the potential to thrive in this role and by supporting them with their career progression. Florence is one of our
staff we are investing in, to become a leader within head and neck dietetics. Florence is currently completing a
Chief Nurse Fellowship programme, similar to the NIHR PCAF, in time and on budget according to her planned
programme of work.
UCLH is one of the founding members of the biomedical research centres and continues to lead on ground-
breaking advances in research for the benefits of patients and sharing best practise with its academic partner
UCL. AHP clinical academics are essential for BRC funded institutions. Florence’s proposed fellowship will be
based across UCL and UCLH, aligning with UCLH Strategy for 2023-27 to deepen collaboration between
UCLH/UCL and broaden the workforce and career opportunities for NMAHPs to improve the di
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_t.text:
versity in expertise
and knowledge across different healthcare professionals. This aligns with the recent NHS Long-term workforce
plan which includes developing NMAHP clinical academics to bridge healthcare providers with academic
institutions and improve the diversity and inclusion of our underrepresented groups in all aspects of healthcare
including research.
Should Florence’s application be successful, we will continue this support through our peer support programme for
doctoral and clinical academic NMAHPs. She will have access to the CNMAR Research Education Framework,
incorporating workshops and lectures delivered by clinical academic staff to equip NMAHPs with the practical skills
and ethical guidance for conducting research with integrity in complex, healthcare settings. Florence will also be
encouraged and supported to access this additional training, which aims to apply theoretical research training into
clinical practice.
Members of our peer support programme are ambassadors for inclusion and equality in research and our
commitment to diversity in research is discussed in all meetings to inform new collaborations and research
outputs. UCLH is certified by the Internation
sectdars__001_t.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_u.text:
al Disabilities Management Standards Council. Delivery of equality
objectives are monitored and supported by the Diversity and Equality Steering Group. Members include
representatives from the Black Asian Minority Ethnic (BAME) network. The BAME and LGBTQ+ networks focus on
empowering staff to achieve their potential, influencing UCLH policy and supporting progression in the workforce.
We adhere to the NHS Equality Delivery System 2 (EDS2) to support inclusive leadership and promote workforce
race equality standards (WRES).
UCLH will comply with the relevant Principles and Obligations for clinical academic training.
HEI partner organisation
Florence has demonstrated her commitment and dedication towards a clinical academic career through the
development of this proposal which is rooted in her clinical dietetic background in head and neck cancer. She has
exceptional potential, evidenced by her strong academic achievement at both undergraduate and postgraduate
level and outputs during her clinical career and winning two pre-doctoral fellowships.
Florence will be based in the Research Department for Targeted Intervention (RDTI), within the Division of Surgery
and Interventional Scienc
sectdars__001_u.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_v.text:
e (DSIS), in the Faculty of Medical Sciences at UCL. Our department provides an
excellent environment for early and mid-career researchers, particularly clinical academics. UCL, and specifically
DSIS, is committed to promoting equality, diversity and inclusion, and holds a silver Athena Swan award. Within
DSIS, we currently have over 100 doctoral research students, and RDTI hosts the largest proportion of these
across our three campuses. RDTI is host to seven thriving research centres, each including clinician and career
scientists, covering a range of research portfolios from discovery science through to policy and implementation
research. Our team includes several clinician scientists from allied health professions who will be able to provide
mentorship to Florence as she progresses her studies. RDTI currently hosts several awards from NIHR and UKRI
including an NIHR Research Professor, an MRC Senior Fellow and, in conjunction with UCLH, the NIHR Central
London Patient Safety Research Collaboration (CL-PSRC). Furthermore, the RDTI leadership team include two
theme leads at the UCLH NIHR Biomedical Research Centre, demonstrating our strong working relationship with
our main NHS pa
sectdars__001_v.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_w.text:
rtner.
Florence’s proposal is well aligned with the strategic direction of the division. She will specifically be based within
the UCL Head and Neck Academic Centre (HNAC) and the Centre for Perioperative Medicine (CPOM) both based
at RDTI. HNAC is the academic arm of the UCLH head and neck cancer unit, aligning with Florence’s clinical
speciality. Florence will also be supported by the NIHR CL-PSRC, with access to the PSRC learning academy
which currently includes five doctoral and three post-doctoral scientists, and which provides mentoring and peer
support from a multidisciplinary team including frontline clinicians, policy makers and academics with a diverse skill
set. The Rapid Research Evaluation and Appraisal Lab (RREAL) also sits within RDTI, and is led by Professor
Vindrola-Padros, Florence’s secondary supervisor. RREAL is uniquely placed to support Florence with the rapid
methodologies she is proposing to undertake. UCL also has a rapidly growing Nutritional Sciences department
within the Division of Medicine which recently opened a pre-registration MSc degree in Dietetics (2021). Florence
will be able to access the expertise, training and equipment available from this de
sectdars__001_w.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_x.text:
partment to support her during
her DCAF. Finally, Florence can exploit the extensive skills development programme for staff and students at all
levels at UCL, through the UCL doctoral school, and the wider offer from UCL with over 170 live or self-paced
courses encompassing: leadership; engagement; influence and impact; knowledge and intellectual abilities;
personal effectiveness; and research governance.

Justification of Costs
Professor Ambler is Professor in Medical Statistics at UCL with research interests and expertise in regression
analysis and developing prediction models. Professor Ambler has been advising me on the statistical analysis plan
for my cohort study in this proposal. This includes complex regression analysis which goes beyond the scope of a
typical PhD student. I

for 6 months costing is equivalent to 2 weeks of his time, which will be spread out during the analysis phase of my
cohort study.

Please describe fully: Subsistence (vouchers for time taken for PPI consultation groups)
Justification of Cost: Each consultation group will be for 2 hrs. There will be 2 x 2hr PPI consultation groups per
year for 5 PPI members. Costs to cover time estimated at £25/hr per NIHR INVOLVE guidance: Year 1: 2 x PPI
consultation group discussions (2hrs each at £25/hr for 5 PPI members = £500) 1) Recap of research project plan,
review scoping review search terms and advise on grey literature 2) Reviewing survey study and questions. Year
2: 2 x PPI consultation group discussions (2hrs each at £25/hr for 5 PPI members = £500) 1) Advise on cohort
study outcome measures e.g. diet diary format and instructions/example page 2) Reviewing qualitative study
including feedback on interview questions and participant information sheets. Year 3: 2x PPI consultation group
discussions (2hrs each at £25/hr for 5 PPI members = £500) 1) Feedback on qualitative enquiry transcripts 2)
Discuss results of all studies, design toolkit and discuss dissemination of research findings via a co-produced
article and to facilitate presentation skills training.

implications: Applicant CV: Lists multiple research grants (NIHR, UCLH) and formal training (Biostatistics, MRes module), demonstrating relevant research experience and training.
Applicant Research Background: Details specific research methods (SPSS, thematic analysis, audits, clinical trials) and formal training (MRes, MSc), strongly supporting g.3.

Training & Development and Research Support: Details prior experience in quantitative research and statistics, while outlining a robust plan to acquire missing skills in qualitative methods and complex intervention desig
Applicant CV: Extensive publication record (10+ papers) and awards (Best Abstract, Dean's List) confirm impactful research outputs and recognitions.
Applicant Research Background: Lists multiple publications in peer-reviewed journals, conference presentations, and awards (Best Abstract, Dean's List), confirming impactful outputs.
Applicant CV: Progression from Band 5 to Band 8a Clinical Lead and Associate Principal Investigator roles shows increasing responsibility and leadership trajectory.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates increasing responsibility through Associate-PI roles, supervising dissertations, and leading clinical teams, supporting leadership trajectory.

Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly states career ambitions to lead an ERAS Centre of Excellence and includes leadership training (Rosalind Franklin Programme) and mentorship to support this trajector
Applicant CV: Current role as Senior Specialist Dietitian alongside research grants and publications demonstrates a clear practitioner-academic career path.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly identifies as a 'developing clinical-academic' balancing full-time clinical work with research, strongly supporting g.6.

Training & Development and Research Support: Demonstrates commitment to a practitioner-academic career through planned clinical practice (1 day/fortnight), clinical governance meetings, and maintaining specialist dietiti
Applicant CV: High volume of peer-reviewed publications and successful grant applications indicate strong quality of previous research.
Applicant Research Background: Provides concrete examples of successful prior projects (service evaluations, audits, trials) and high-quality publications, supporting pr.8.
Applicant CV: Secured a PPI starter-grant to form a group and conduct discussions, showing prior integration of lived experience in research design.
Applicant Research Background: Mentions securing a PPI starter-grant and incorporating patient feedback into proposal development, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes specific co-design activities where patients checked information sheets and questions, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions stakeholder consultation for toolkit development and PPI advice for dissemination materials, supporting wp.1.
Detailed Research Plan: Details PPI involvement in review consultation, survey design, and toolkit development, confirming integration of lived experience, supporting wp.1.

Patient & Public Involvement: Details specific design changes (e.g., outcome measures, interview questions, staff inclusion) based on PPI feedback, strongly supporting integration of lived experience.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly states a vision to improve nutritional care in HNC and a trajectory to become a clinical research leader, supporting g.1.

Training & Development and Research Support: Clearly articulates a vision to become a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading a research group, with a detailed training plan supporting this trajectory.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly identifies the fellowship as the 'crucial next step' to transition from developing academic to research leader, supporting g.2.

Training & Development and Research Support: Identifies specific skill gaps (e.g., PPI facilitation, scoping reviews, qualitative methods) and maps targeted training to bridge them, justifying the award as the necessary
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly outlines the problem, aim, methods, and impact in accessible language, supporting pr.1.

Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show high readability (Flesch-Kincaid 6.34, 65.47 ease), low jargon density (0.98%), and coverage of key elements (problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, impact), strongly su
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Plain English Summary and NLP analysis sections, indicating structural compliance for readability and content checks.
Plain English Summary of Research: Cites previous research statistics (40-72% patients) and identifies a gap in current practice regarding tube removal timing, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Identifies knowledge gaps regarding optimal feeding practices and proposes a scoping review to map current literature, supporting pr.2.
Detailed Research Plan: Critically reviews existing literature, identifying specific gaps such as selection bias, non-validated measures, and lack of data on protein intake, strongly supporting pr.2.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes a feasible multi-stage approach (review, survey, data collection) across 65 hospitals, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines a feasible 3-year mixed-methods design with specific sample sizes (n=65, n=90) and five workstreams, supporting pr.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines a feasible 36-month project with 5 workstreams, specific sample sizes (n=90), and realistic recruitment targets, supporting pr.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly details methods including literature review, staff surveys, and hospital record analysis, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Details rigorous methods including scoping review, cross-sectional survey, prospective cohort with regression analysis, and qualitative framework analysis, supporting pr.4.
Detailed Research Plan: Provides explicit methodological details including Arksey & O'Malley framework, PG-SGA outcomes, regression models, and sample size justification, supporting pr.4.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms a 'Detailed Research Plan' (5532 words) with structured headings, indicating comprehensive methodological description.
Plain English Summary of Research: Highlights direct patient benefits (recovery, wound healing) and NHS cost savings, supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly lists impacts on patients (nutrition, swallowing), professionals (toolkit), and NHS (reduced stays/costs), supporting pr.5.
Detailed Research Plan: Quantifies patient benefits (reduced malnutrition, improved QoL) and economic impact (£90k-£13m savings), clearly supporting pr.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details patient involvement across all stages (design, data collection, review), supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Includes patient interviews, stakeholder consultation for toolkit development, and PPI advice for dissemination, supporting pr.6.
Detailed Research Plan: Integrates PPI in review consultation, survey development, and toolkit refinement, demonstrating strong engagement with people and communities, supporting pr.6.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms a dedicated 'Patient & Public Involvement' section (804 words), indicating engagement strategies are explicitly addressed.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines dissemination plans via social media, blogs, and animated videos for patients, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Describes dissemination via journals, conferences, social media, and animations tailored to patient audiences, supporting wp.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines comprehensive dissemination plans including patient animations, social media, and professional conferences, supporting wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines co-production of manuscripts, attendance at patient conferences, and dissemination strategies tailored to patient groups, supporting maximising public benefit.

Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly plans for diverse recruitment (ethnicity, age, disease stage) and includes costed interpreters to ensure inclusivity, supporting pr.9.

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision to improve nutritional care in Head and Neck Cancer (HNC) and a distinct career trajectory towards becoming a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading an ERAS Centre of Excellence. The training plan is exceptionally well-justified, mapping specific courses (e.g., JBI scoping review, NVivo, Implementation Science) to identified skill gaps. Strong mentorship is evidenced by a supervisory team with extensive publication records and leadership roles in relevant fields.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' regarding the intervention's novelty could be slightly more emphasized against existing ERAS protocols, though the mixed-methods approach provides sufficient differentiation.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' regarding the intervention's novelty could be slightly more emphasized against existing ERAS protocols, though the mixed-methods approach provides sufficient differentiation.

DT27179 - HCPC
Please describe your research career to date
I am a dietitian and developing clinical-academic who strives to improve the nutritional care of patients with head
and neck cancer (HNC) through applying evidence-based nutritional interventions into practice. Witnessing the
positive impact nutrition has on physical/psychosocial wellbeing motivates me to pursue an independent role in
research and contribute new knowledge to improve/innovate nutritional care.
Research experience
My enthusiasm for research started during my undergraduate dissertation which investigated the diets of university
athletes. I gained ethical approval, recruited six participants, collected dietary data, and explored dietary choices
through a focus group. I developed skills in statistical (using SPSS) and

agement skills through seconding as Clinical-Lead HNC Dietitian twice (over
2-years). My responsibilities included team management, complex workforce planning amid COVID-19, staff
recruitment/retention, developing business cases and utilising research funding within a clinical service. I have
contributed to coroners’ inquests, instilling in me the importance of evidence when justifying clinical decisions.
I am applying for this fellowship as though I have made great progress as a developing clinical-academic, this is
the crucial next step in becoming a clinical research leader. My ambitions include leading a research programme
in perioperative nutrition at UCL, supporting UCLH become an ERAS Centre of Excellence and nurturing dietetic
talent to produce a sustainable team of clinical academics.
Have you already
No
registered for a PhD?
Has this application, or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
No
Contextual Factors

assessment of

Proposed training and development programme
My existing research experience helped me develop skills in quantitative research, trial delivery, dissemination,
and academic writing for publication. The Vitae-RDF helped me identify gaps in my learning to deliver the
proposed studies alongside clinical practice training and placements. My training plan supports my long-term
aspirations of becoming a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading a research group in perioperative
nutrition at UCL and improving ERAS at UCLH. The GANTT chart included indicates key milestones in my training
programme.
Proposed formal study and academic training
As a PhD student at UCL, I will access the comprehensive doctoral skills development programme which includes
PhD training modules such as preparing a thesis. I will also attend courses on Data Management, Zotero, Creating
Infographics and Designing an Impactful Poster to equip me with secure data collection, referencing management,
and dissemination. Whilst I have undergone several PPI workshops, I found facilitating my group challenging and
would benefit from further training through undertaking the Facilitation of PPI activities workshop.
To deve
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ss, at KCL to develop my knowledge of implementation planning, development, and evaluation. I will also
undertake the Influencing and Negotiating course at UCL to effectively manage my stakeholder consultation group.
Clinical academic development
Extend skills in Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS)
My research and clinical interests align well with the topic of ERAS. Current ERAS Centres of Excellence are
mainly led by colorectal surgeons. A gap exists for an HNC-led Centre of Excellence – it is my aspiration to play a
leading role in such a centre at UCLH.
To achieve this, I will attend the ERAS Society Course to develop my knowledge of key ERAS principles and visit
two leading/founding ERAS Centres of Excellence in Stockholm. This includes the Karolinska Institute and Ersta-
Diakoni Hospital, under the supervision of Anna Garpenbeck (ERAS coordinating nurse at Ersta-Diakoni and a
board member of the Swedish ERAS Society). I will also visit Professor Bill Fawcett and Dr Chris Jones
(Consultant Anaesthetists and ERAS Society committee members) at the Royal Surrey Hospital to learn how to
set up an ERAS Centre of Excellence in the UK/NHS.
To develop my wider networks and knowledg

onsultant in
anaesthetics, perioperative and critical care at UCLH. She has held several prominent leadership positions
including: Director, Royal College of Anaesthetists’ Health Services Research Centre (2016-22); academic training
advisor, National Institute for Academic Anaesthesia (2012-19); training programme director for academic
anaesthesia in London (2012-18); Director NIHR Central London Patient Safety Research Collaboration (2023-28);
theme lead, NIHR UCLH Biomedical Research Centre (2022-27); NHS England’s National Clinical Director for
Critical and Perioperative Care (2020-).
At UCL, she is group lead, Centre Lead and head of the Research Department for Targeted Intervention where my
PhD will be based. She currently has six doctoral students; she has previously supervised six clinicians through to
PhD completion; five more are writing up. She has also been a mentor / advisor to NIHR-funded early/mid-career
researchers in Bristol, Birmingham and London.
She has a longstanding relationship with both Professor Vindrola-Padros and Dr Govender, both of whom she has
supported to academic promotion. She leads research which uses multiple methodologies including quantitative,

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly identifies this fellowship as the 'crucial next step' to transition from a developing academic to a research leader, justifying the timing. The award is positioned to bridge specific skill gaps (qualitative methods, PPI facilitation, complex intervention design) that are currently limiting the applicant's ability to lead independent research. The plan details how the award will build partnerships with professional bodies (BDA, BAHNO) and patient groups (Young Tongues).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is robust and directly linked to career progression and project delivery.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is robust and directly linked to career progression and project delivery.

evidence.text:
lop skills in scoping review methodology, I will attend the online JBI Scoping review workshop at University
of Nottingham and access 1:1 support from a research librarian at UCL to develop my search strategy. I will also
undertake two modules at UCL in Survey Design and Quantitative Survey Research to equip me with design,
conduct and analysis of survey data.
I have previously undertaken quantitative research and completed the UCL Practical Medical Statistics Course
during my pre-doctoral fellowship which has provided a good baseline training in statistics and software (SPSS
and Stata). I will undertake the following courses at UCL to further develop these skills for analysing my cohort
study data: Statistics for observational studies, Regression Analysis and Introduction to R. I have prior experience
using Diet-Plan software for analysing diet diaries, but plan to use Nutritics as this is more robust. Professor
Nathan Davies (Dietetics programme lead at UCL) has agreed to provide me with a license free-of-charge and
attend his practical classes using this software.
Qualitative research methods comprise a key component of this project and I will invest time in developing and
expan
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ding my knowledge and skills in this area. To develop baseline knowledge, I will attend the Qualitative
Research Methods in Health short-course at UCL which includes practical skills for conducting interviews. I will
also undertake the Rapid Research Evaluation and Appraisal Bootcamp at UCL to equip me with using the rapid
methods I am proposing. Having not conducted any previous participant observations, I will attend the
Ethnography for Healthcare Improvement Summer School at University of Leicester. These courses do not include
training on software for coding/analysis, therefore I will also undertake the Introduction to NVivo short-course at
University of Surrey. Lastly, whilst I have published quantitative research, I have not published qualitative research
therefore I will attend the Writing/Publishing Qualitative Research course at UCL.
Finally, central to my research is the development of a toolkit anticipated to be a complex intervention drawing on
implementation science theories. I will therefore attend the Mixed-Methods approaches course at UCL, Developing
and Evaluating Complex Interventions, online-course at University of Glasgow and the Implementation Science
Mastercla

Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.drawbacks: None identified; the evidence of prior experience and training is comprehensive and directly relevant to the proposed study's requirements.

I am a dietitian and developing clinical-academic who strives to improve the nutritional care of patients with head

athletes. I gained ethical approval, recruited six participants, collected dietary data, and explored dietary choices
through a focus group. I developed skills in statistical (using SPSS) and

Throughout my career, I have developed research skills in quantitative data analysis, dissemination (journal
publications and conference presentations), and stakeholder engagement through conducting 10 service
evaluations and audits to resolve problems encountered in day-to-day practice. The satisfaction gained from
seeing direct improvements in care makes me appreciate pursuing research within a clinical role. For example,
following concerns that the local protocol for gastrostomy insertion caused unnecessary placement delays, I
audited complications, benchmarked pathways, and formed a stakeholder group to agree policy changes. A re-
audit revealed reduced delays without increased complications, published in Clinical Nutrition-OpenScience.
My interest in enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) began with a service evaluation revealing patients wait for

sub_criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant demonstrates a clear trajectory towards research leadership, progressing from Band 5 to Band 8a Clinical Lead and Associate Principal Investigator roles. They have taken on increasing responsibility by supervising dissertations and leading clinical teams. The training plan includes specific leadership development (Rosalind Franklin Programme) and mentorship to support the goal of establishing an ERAS Centre of Excellence.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the trajectory is strong, the specific 'recognition by peers' (g.5.d) is implied through awards and grants but could be more explicitly stated as external validation of leadership potential beyond the institution.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the trajectory is strong, the specific 'recognition by peers' (g.5.d) is implied through awards and grants but could be more explicitly stated as external validation of leadership potential beyond the institution.

he NIHR
Clinical-Academic pathway presented the ideal opportunity to provide training/resources towards doctorate and
beyond, steering me to successfully apply for an NIHR-Health Education England (HEE) pre-doctoral bridging
award (November 2021). This important milestone included an MRes module in PhD-Proposal Development and
helped me develop my ideas through academic supervision and patient feedback, after I was awarded a PPI
starter-grant. I was then awarded a UCLH Chief-Nurse Research Fellowship (June 2022), to develop a doctoral
application, undertake statistics training, and improve my publication outputs.
As a developing clinical-academic, I have taken opportunities to acquire research skills whilst in practice. I am an
NIHR-accredited Associate-PI on SIPSMART2 prehabilitation trial. Alongside learning all aspects of clinical trial
delivery, I successfully led recruitment at UCLH to target, recruiting 33% of eligible participants. I showcased my
research skills in Advances in Communication and Swallowing, demonstrating development opportunities across
all Vitae-RDF domains. I demonstrated capability of allied health professionals in leading trial delivery with fellow
Associ

esigned, and revalidated a 30-credit dietetics module. I was personal tutor to five
students, providing pastoral care.
I represent dietetics on HEE Aspirant Cancer-Career and Education-Development programme (workstream-1),
reviewing e-learning modules in cancer-care, which increased knowledge and understanding amongst aspirant
cancer professionals (published in British Journal of Nursing; co-author).
I have successfully supervised 6 MSc and 2 BSc dissertations to completion. One student audited weight change
during radiotherapy. This raised awareness of weight loss risks amongst patients with obesity. We published the
abstract in Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics and were awarded the ‘Best Abstract Prize’ at the BDA
Research Symposium.
Professional experience
I have developed specialist skills in HNC through advanced communication training and MSc Cancer-Care
modules (Royal Marsden School). I have extensive clinical supervision experience, having developed staff-training
competencies in HNC. I was the department’s student-training co-lead 2019-2020 and helped set-up the
departments research group to develop a research culture in dietetics.
I have developed leadership and man

agement skills through seconding as Clinical-Lead HNC Dietitian twice (over
2-years). My responsibilities included team management, complex workforce planning amid COVID-19, staff
recruitment/retention, developing business cases and utilising research funding within a clinical service. I have
contributed to coroners’ inquests, instilling in me the importance of evidence when justifying clinical decisions.
I am applying for this fellowship as though I have made great progress as a developing clinical-academic, this is
the crucial next step in becoming a clinical research leader. My ambitions include leading a research programme
in perioperative nutrition at UCL, supporting UCLH become an ERAS Centre of Excellence and nurturing dietetic
talent to produce a sustainable team of clinical academics.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly identifies as a 'developing clinical-academic' and holds a current role as Senior Specialist Dietitian alongside research activities. The proposal includes a clear plan to balance practice (1 day/fortnight) with research, maintaining clinical governance and specialist dietitian status. They are actively involved in professional networks (BDA, BAHNO) and have secured PPI starter grants, demonstrating a commitment to the practitioner-academic model.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the commitment to balancing clinical practice and research is well-articulated and supported by the proposed schedule and institutional support.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the commitment to balancing clinical practice and research is well-articulated and supported by the proposed schedule and institutional support.

DT27179 - HCPC
Please describe your research career to date
I am a dietitian and developing clinical-academic who strives to improve the nutritional care of patients with head
and neck cancer (HNC) through applying evidence-based nutritional interventions into practice. Witnessing the
positive impact nutrition has on physical/psychosocial wellbeing motivates me to pursue an independent role in
research and contribute new knowledge to improve/innovate nutritional care.
Research experience
My enthusiasm for research started during my undergraduate dissertation which investigated the diets of university
athletes. I gained ethical approval, recruited six participants, collected dietary data, and explored dietary choices
through a focus group. I developed skills in statistical (using SPSS) and

thematic analysis.
Throughout my career, I have developed research skills in quantitative data analysis, dissemination (journal
publications and conference presentations), and stakeholder engagement through conducting 10 service
evaluations and audits to resolve problems encountered in day-to-day practice. The satisfaction gained from
seeing direct improvements in care makes me appreciate pursuing research within a clinical role. For example,
following concerns that the local protocol for gastrostomy insertion caused unnecessary placement delays, I
audited complications, benchmarked pathways, and formed a stakeholder group to agree policy changes. A re-
audit revealed reduced delays without increased complications, published in Clinical Nutrition-OpenScience.
My interest in enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) began with a service evaluation revealing patients wait for
prolonged periods before recommencing oral intake post-surgery. I published findings in Oral Oncology Reports
and presented at the British Association of Head and Neck Oncologists (BAHNO) conference. I realised that
evidence-gaps were best answered through research and could change perioperative nutritional care. T

ate-PIs, at the BAHNO conference. I was invited to share my experience at the NIHR conference:
Research, the NHS and you; improving awareness of clinical research opportunities accessible in practice.
Furthermore, when a studentship for MSc Clinical Nutrition tuition-fees was advertised, I successfully applied and
completed alongside full-time clinical work which included taught modules in ‘Research Methods’. I developed
academic writing skills through my dissertation, which was a quasi-experimental study assessing participant
adherence with intermittent-fasting diets, published in Clinical Nutrition-ESPEN. I was awarded the UCL Dean’s
List Commendation in recognition of outstanding academic achievement.
Collaboration and professional engagement
I have developed national and international networks though election to the BAHNO-Council (representing
dietetics) and BAHNO-International Collaboration Committee. I have helped organise four annual BAHNO
conferences to-date by marking abstracts and chairing sessions.
I co-authored national standards published in Journal of Oral Pathology; the benchmark document for quality
assurance in HNC used nationally and abroad. I led ENT-UK national

criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision to improve nutritional care in Head and Neck Cancer (HNC) and a distinct career trajectory towards becoming a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading an ERAS Centre of Excellence. The training plan is exceptionally well-justified, mapping specific courses (e.g., JBI scoping review, NVivo, Implementation Science) to identified skill gaps. Strong mentorship is evidenced by a supervisory team with extensive publication records and leadership roles in relevant fields.
criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' regarding the intervention's novelty could be slightly more emphasized against existing ERAS protocols, though the mixed-methods approach provides sufficient differentiation.
criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive ideas' regarding the intervention's novelty could be slightly more emphasized against existing ERAS protocols, though the mixed-methods approach provides sufficient differentiation.

Proposed training and development programme
My existing research experience helped me develop skills in quantitative research, trial delivery, dissemination,
and academic writing for publication. The Vitae-RDF helped me identify gaps in my learning to deliver the
proposed studies alongside clinical practice training and placements. My training plan supports my long-term
aspirations of becoming a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading a research group in perioperative
nutrition at UCL and improving ERAS at UCLH. The GANTT chart included indicates key milestones in my training
programme.
Proposed formal study and academic training
As a PhD student at UCL, I will access the comprehensive doctoral skills development programme which includes
PhD training modules such as preparing a thesis. I will also attend courses on Data Management, Zotero, Creating
Infographics and Designing an Impactful Poster to equip me with secure data collection, referencing management,
and dissemination. Whilst I have undergone several PPI workshops, I found facilitating my group challenging and
would benefit from further training through undertaking the Facilitation of PPI activities workshop.
To deve

evidence.text:
ss, at KCL to develop my knowledge of implementation planning, development, and evaluation. I will also
undertake the Influencing and Negotiating course at UCL to effectively manage my stakeholder consultation group.
Clinical academic development
Extend skills in Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS)
My research and clinical interests align well with the topic of ERAS. Current ERAS Centres of Excellence are
mainly led by colorectal surgeons. A gap exists for an HNC-led Centre of Excellence – it is my aspiration to play a
leading role in such a centre at UCLH.
To achieve this, I will attend the ERAS Society Course to develop my knowledge of key ERAS principles and visit
two leading/founding ERAS Centres of Excellence in Stockholm. This includes the Karolinska Institute and Ersta-
Diakoni Hospital, under the supervision of Anna Garpenbeck (ERAS coordinating nurse at Ersta-Diakoni and a
board member of the Swedish ERAS Society). I will also visit Professor Bill Fawcett and Dr Chris Jones
(Consultant Anaesthetists and ERAS Society committee members) at the Royal Surrey Hospital to learn how to
set up an ERAS Centre of Excellence in the UK/NHS.
To develop my wider networks and knowledg

advisor, National Institute for Academic Anaesthesia (2012-19); training programme director for academic
anaesthesia in London (2012-18); Director NIHR Central London Patient Safety Research Collaboration (2023-28);

At UCL, she is group lead, Centre Lead and head of the Research Department for Targeted Intervention where my

She has a longstanding relationship with both Professor Vindrola-Padros and Dr Govender, both of whom she has

criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly identifies this fellowship as the 'crucial next step' to transition from a developing academic to a research leader, justifying the timing. The award is positioned to bridge specific skill gaps (qualitative methods, PPI facilitation, complex intervention design) that are currently limiting the applicant's ability to lead independent research. The plan details how the award will build partnerships with professional bodies (BDA, BAHNO) and patient groups (Young Tongues).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is robust and directly linked to career progression and project delivery.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is robust and directly linked to career progression and project delivery.

lop skills in scoping review methodology, I will attend the online JBI Scoping review workshop at University
of Nottingham and access 1:1 support from a research librarian at UCL to develop my search strategy. I will also
undertake two modules at UCL in Survey Design and Quantitative Survey Research to equip me with design,
conduct and analysis of survey data.
I have previously undertaken quantitative research and completed the UCL Practical Medical Statistics Course
during my pre-doctoral fellowship which has provided a good baseline training in statistics and software (SPSS
and Stata). I will undertake the following courses at UCL to further develop these skills for analysing my cohort
study data: Statistics for observational studies, Regression Analysis and Introduction to R. I have prior experience
using Diet-Plan software for analysing diet diaries, but plan to use Nutritics as this is more robust. Professor
Nathan Davies (Dietetics programme lead at UCL) has agreed to provide me with a license free-of-charge and
attend his practical classes using this software.
Qualitative research methods comprise a key component of this project and I will invest time in developing and
expan
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ding my knowledge and skills in this area. To develop baseline knowledge, I will attend the Qualitative
Research Methods in Health short-course at UCL which includes practical skills for conducting interviews. I will
also undertake the Rapid Research Evaluation and Appraisal Bootcamp at UCL to equip me with using the rapid
methods I am proposing. Having not conducted any previous participant observations, I will attend the
Ethnography for Healthcare Improvement Summer School at University of Leicester. These courses do not include
training on software for coding/analysis, therefore I will also undertake the Introduction to NVivo short-course at
University of Surrey. Lastly, whilst I have published quantitative research, I have not published qualitative research
therefore I will attend the Writing/Publishing Qualitative Research course at UCL.
Finally, central to my research is the development of a toolkit anticipated to be a complex intervention drawing on
implementation science theories. I will therefore attend the Mixed-Methods approaches course at UCL, Developing
and Evaluating Complex Interventions, online-course at University of Glasgow and the Implementation Science

criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

criteria.pros: The applicant has extensive prior experience with relevant research methods (SPSS, thematic analysis, audits, clinical trials) and formal training (MRes, MSc, Biostatistics). The CV lists multiple successful grants (NIHR, UCLH) and a strong publication record. There is a clear alignment between this prior quantitative experience and the need to acquire qualitative skills for the proposed mixed-methods study.
criteria.drawbacks: None identified; the evidence of prior experience and training is comprehensive and directly relevant to the proposed study's requirements.
criteria.rationale: None identified; the evidence of prior experience and training is comprehensive and directly relevant to the proposed study's requirements.

he NIHR
Clinical-Academic pathway presented the ideal opportunity to provide training/resources towards doctorate and
beyond, steering me to successfully apply for an NIHR-Health Education England (HEE) pre-doctoral bridging
award (November 2021). This important milestone included an MRes module in PhD-Proposal Development and
helped me develop my ideas through academic supervision and patient feedback, after I was awarded a PPI
starter-grant. I was then awarded a UCLH Chief-Nurse Research Fellowship (June 2022), to develop a doctoral
application, undertake statistics training, and improve my publication outputs.
As a developing clinical-academic, I have taken opportunities to acquire research skills whilst in practice. I am an
NIHR-accredited Associate-PI on SIPSMART2 prehabilitation trial. Alongside learning all aspects of clinical trial
delivery, I successfully led recruitment at UCLH to target, recruiting 33% of eligible participants. I showcased my
research skills in Advances in Communication and Swallowing, demonstrating development opportunities across
all Vitae-RDF domains. I demonstrated capability of allied health professionals in leading trial delivery with fellow
Associ

esigned, and revalidated a 30-credit dietetics module. I was personal tutor to five
students, providing pastoral care.
I represent dietetics on HEE Aspirant Cancer-Career and Education-Development programme (workstream-1),
reviewing e-learning modules in cancer-care, which increased knowledge and understanding amongst aspirant
cancer professionals (published in British Journal of Nursing; co-author).
I have successfully supervised 6 MSc and 2 BSc dissertations to completion. One student audited weight change
during radiotherapy. This raised awareness of weight loss risks amongst patients with obesity. We published the
abstract in Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics and were awarded the ‘Best Abstract Prize’ at the BDA
Research Symposium.
Professional experience
I have developed specialist skills in HNC through advanced communication training and MSc Cancer-Care
modules (Royal Marsden School). I have extensive clinical supervision experience, having developed staff-training
competencies in HNC. I was the department’s student-training co-lead 2019-2020 and helped set-up the
departments research group to develop a research culture in dietetics.
I have developed leadership and man

agement skills through seconding as Clinical-Lead HNC Dietitian twice (over
2-years). My responsibilities included team management, complex workforce planning amid COVID-19, staff
recruitment/retention, developing business cases and utilising research funding within a clinical service. I have
contributed to coroners’ inquests, instilling in me the importance of evidence when justifying clinical decisions.
I am applying for this fellowship as though I have made great progress as a developing clinical-academic, this is
the crucial next step in becoming a clinical research leader. My ambitions include leading a research programme
in perioperative nutrition at UCL, supporting UCLH become an ERAS Centre of Excellence and nurturing dietetic
talent to produce a sustainable team of clinical academics.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)

Alignment with detailed proposal content

Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary demonstrates exceptional readability with a Flesch-Kincaid grade of 6.34 and low jargon density (0.98%). It clearly articulates the problem (malnutrition in HNC), objectives, methods (review, survey, cohort), beneficiaries, and real-world impact (cost savings, improved recovery) in accessible language.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the summary is excellent, the lexical overlap with the detailed plan is relatively low (0.118), suggesting the summary is highly condensed, though this is typical for lay summaries and does not detract from its clarity.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the summary is excellent, the lexical overlap with the detailed plan is relatively low (0.118), suggesting the summary is highly condensed, though this is typical for lay summaries and does not detract from its clarity.

evidence.text:
Aim:
This study aims to improve the nutrition of patients with head and neck cancer when moving from tube-feeding to
eating by mouth, after surgery.
Why is this research important?
In the UK, 12,400 people are diagnosed with head and neck cancer each year, of which 75% have surgery. This
may involve removing parts of the mouth used for eating/drinking, such as the tongue. The surgeons will rebuild
these by using tissue from another part of the body, such as the arm. This is called a ‘flap’.
After surgery, patients do not eat or drink for six to twelve days, and nutrition is provided by feeding tube either
through the nose or directly into the stomach. The type of tube placed, the timeframe for re-starting eating/drinking
and when the tube can be removed depends on many factors. These include being able to confidently swallow
enough food/drink safely (affected by the amount of swelling in the mouth) and local hospital policies for when
tubes are removed. Generally, the surgeon and team decide this for each person. However, nose tubes are often
removed as soon as eating/drinking has begun, and before patients can manage enough food/drink. This happens
because these tubes can prevent
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
going home in some hospitals. Previous research shows 40-72% patients do not
eat/drink enough after surgery. Nutrition drastically affects recovery and is the third largest cost-save to the NHS.
Good nutrition speeds up recovery, improves wound healing, survival, and wellbeing. Poor nutrition slows
recovery, increases risks of infection and even death.
How will I do this?
We want to understand what factors help or stop patients restart eating/drinking enough so that feeding tubes can
be removed at the right time. Also, we think some patients can eat/drink sooner. This will improve their nutrition
and help swallowing muscles recover.
 We will first review research articles that have studied patients moving from tube-feeding to
eating/drinking.
 We will then survey healthcare staff including surgeons, nurses, dietitians, and speech therapists across
sixty-five UK hospitals. We will find out how practices differ including when patients restart eating/drinking
and what types of tubes patients go home with.
 We will use the information from the survey and articles to decide what data we will collect from hospital
records. We will also decide which staff practices we will watch and qu
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
estions we need to ask patients and
staff.
When it comes to eating/drinking, the hospital data will tell us when patients restart, whether they have enough,
and reasons for delayed or not enough eating/drinking. The questions and practices watched will help us
understand how patients find eating/drinking after surgery and how staff help them.
Findings will be used to develop a staff toolkit to improve feeding practices and nutrition after surgery.
How have patients been involved?
We discussed this project with patients who have had flap surgery. They told us that this research felt meaningful
to them, and they lacked confidence with restarting eating/drinking as this often occurred abruptly and with little
support. This made them feel isolated. We will work with patients across all stages of this project to:
 Alert us to missed areas.
 Check study information sheets are clear.
 Check the data we plan to collect.
 Check questions we will ask patients and staff to ensure these are clear/relevant.
 Review patient interview logs to check we understand the patients’ viewpoint.
How will findings be shared?
We will share with patients through social media, blogs, our funder's website

sub_criteria.pros: The application critically reviews existing literature, identifying specific gaps such as selection bias, non-validated measures, and lack of data on protein intake. It proposes a scoping review to map current evidence and highlights the discrepancy between guidelines and practice (40-72% of patients affected).

sub_criteria.rationale: The specific number of references cited is not explicitly quantified in the provided text, though the critical analysis of the literature is robust.

evidence.text:
Aim:
This study aims to improve the nutrition of patients with head and neck cancer when moving from tube-feeding to
eating by mouth, after surgery.
Why is this research important?
In the UK, 12,400 people are diagnosed with head and neck cancer each year, of which 75% have surgery. This
may involve removing parts of the mouth used for eating/drinking, such as the tongue. The surgeons will rebuild
these by using tissue from another part of the body, such as the arm. This is called a ‘flap’.
After surgery, patients do not eat or drink for six to twelve days, and nutrition is provided by feeding tube either
through the nose or directly into the stomach. The type of tube placed, the timeframe for re-starting eating/drinking
and when the tube can be removed depends on many factors. These include being able to confidently swallow
enough food/drink safely (affected by the amount of swelling in the mouth) and local hospital policies for when
tubes are removed. Generally, the surgeon and team decide this for each person. However, nose tubes are often
removed as soon as eating/drinking has begun, and before patients can manage enough food/drink. This happens
because these tubes can prevent

evidence.text:
going home in some hospitals. Previous research shows 40-72% patients do not
eat/drink enough after surgery. Nutrition drastically affects recovery and is the third largest cost-save to the NHS.
Good nutrition speeds up recovery, improves wound healing, survival, and wellbeing. Poor nutrition slows
recovery, increases risks of infection and even death.
How will I do this?
We want to understand what factors help or stop patients restart eating/drinking enough so that feeding tubes can
be removed at the right time. Also, we think some patients can eat/drink sooner. This will improve their nutrition
and help swallowing muscles recover.
 We will first review research articles that have studied patients moving from tube-feeding to
eating/drinking.
 We will then survey healthcare staff including surgeons, nurses, dietitians, and speech therapists across
sixty-five UK hospitals. We will find out how practices differ including when patients restart eating/drinking
and what types of tubes patients go home with.
 We will use the information from the survey and articles to decide what data we will collect from hospital
records. We will also decide which staff practices we will watch and qu

evidence.text:
Background
Surgery is a widely accepted treatment for head and neck cancer (HNC). After surgery, patients are made ‘nil-by-
mouth’ for approximately 6-12 days to enable wound healing and nutrition is provided via a feeding tube placed
through the nose (naso-gastric tube) or directly into the stomach (gastrostomy). Variation exists for time to first oral
intake, type of feeding tube placed and when these are removed, with limited evidence to determine optimal
practice. Many patients experience delays in recommencing oral intake which deconditions swallowing
musculature. During the transition from tube to oral intake, 40-72% of patients have inadequate nutrition due to
premature removal of feeding tubes. Optimising nutrition is critical in supporting post-operative recovery and
improves wound healing, survival, and quality-of-life.
Research question
Development of a toolkit to improve post-operative feeding practices and nutritional intake in patients with HNC
undergoing surgery with flap tissue reconstruction.
Aims and objectives
This research aims to investigate post-operative feeding practices for patients with HNC undergoing surgery when
transitioning from tube to oral intake and

to develop a staff toolkit to improve timing/adequacy of intake. The nature
of the toolkit will be determined by conducting exploratory work as part of evidence synthesis. This will be
achieved by examining the literature, describing current practice, investigating nutritional outcome data, exploring
patient experiences, staff perceptions and practices.
Methods
A mixed-methods convergent-parallel approach over three years, across five workstreams:
1. Scoping review of existing literature to map current post-operative feeding practices and identify
knowledge gaps necessitating further inquiry.
2. Cross-sectional web-based survey of healthcare professionals working in UK HNC centres (n=65) to map
variation in practices and describe current reality including typical time to first oral intake, type of tube
placed and home tube-feeding status.
3. Prospective observational cohort study (n=90) to investigate nutritional status and intakes during the
transition from tube to oral intake, time/type of first oral intake and associated factors. Multiple regression
analysis to identify predictors of nutritional adequacy.
4. Qualitative approaches using semi-structured interviews and observatio

d NHS costs27.
This research should prevent both developing and/or exacerbating malnutrition through optimising post-operative
nutrition, and decrease readmissions associated with nutrition/hydration but also wound healing, as this is directly
associated with poor nutrition.
Review of existing evidence
Early oral feeding is well established in treatment of other cancer types28 due to benefits including reduced LoS
and improved post-operative nutritional adequacy with calls for high-quality research in HNC29. Five retrospective
studies5,7,9,30,31and one pilot randomised controlled trial (RCT)32have been conducted in HNC flap-surgery with
another RCT registered (NCT04787939). These are limited by inconsistent definitions of early oral feeding (which
a registered scoping review aims to determine33) and selection bias6. Firstly, only patients without a tracheostomy
were randomised to early oral feeding. Tracheostomy-use varies between centres. In centres that treat early-stage
disease, tracheostomy-use is 13%9. In centres that treat more advanced-stage disease, tracheostomy-use is
88%31.Advanced-stage disease makes up ~59% of cases34. Secondly, patients with previous radiotherapy (~30%

evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
estions we need to ask patients and
staff.
When it comes to eating/drinking, the hospital data will tell us when patients restart, whether they have enough,
and reasons for delayed or not enough eating/drinking. The questions and practices watched will help us
understand how patients find eating/drinking after surgery and how staff help them.
Findings will be used to develop a staff toolkit to improve feeding practices and nutrition after surgery.
How have patients been involved?
We discussed this project with patients who have had flap surgery. They told us that this research felt meaningful
to them, and they lacked confidence with restarting eating/drinking as this often occurred abruptly and with little
support. This made them feel isolated. We will work with patients across all stages of this project to:
 Alert us to missed areas.
 Check study information sheets are clear.
 Check the data we plan to collect.
 Check questions we will ask patients and staff to ensure these are clear/relevant.
 Review patient interview logs to check we understand the patients’ viewpoint.
How will findings be shared?
We will share with patients through social media, blogs, our funder's website

of the toolkit will be determined by conducting exploratory work as part of evidence synthesis. This will be
achieved by examining the literature, describing current practice, investigating nutritional outcome data, exploring
patient experiences, staff perceptions and practices.
Methods
A mixed-methods convergent-parallel approach over three years, across five workstreams:
1. Scoping review of existing literature to map current post-operative feeding practices and identify
knowledge gaps necessitating further inquiry.
2. Cross-sectional web-based survey of healthcare professionals working in UK HNC centres (n=65) to map
variation in practices and describe current reality including typical time to first oral intake, type of tube
placed and home tube-feeding status.
3. Prospective observational cohort study (n=90) to investigate nutritional status and intakes during the
transition from tube to oral intake, time/type of first oral intake and associated factors. Multiple regression
analysis to identify predictors of nutritional adequacy.
4. Qualitative approaches using semi-structured interviews and observatio

g flap-surgery (WS2).
3. To examine whether patients meet their nutritional requirements when transitioning from tube to oral
feeding and identify factors predictive of nutritional adequacy and oral feeding commencement (WS3).
4. To explore patient experiences and perceived facilitators and barriers for timely and adequate oral intake
following flap-surgery (WS4).
5. To explore staff perceptions and practices for transitioning patients from tube to oral feeding following flap-
surgery (WS4).
6. To develop a toolkit with stakeholder consultation to optimise post-operative feeding practices and
nutritional intake (WS5).
Project Plan
Underpinning theory
This study is based on the Medical Research Council (MRC) guidance on complex interventions, specifically
focussing on phase 1, intervention development44. This will draw on normalisation process theory (NPT)45to
understand the organisational structures that influence implementation of complex interventions and the theoretical
(TDF)46to
domains framework understand human behaviour in complex clinical environments to improve
implementation.
WS1: Scoping review [Months 1-7]
Rationale:
There are currently no reviews published or registere

mplementable in different units,
and sites for a future multi-centre validation study.
WS3: Prospective cohort study [Months 10-29]
Rationale:
Limited empirical research has identified factors associated with timely/adequate nutritional intake after surgery. A
prospective cohort study will identify factors that may be predictive of nutritional adequacy that can be targeted in
the toolkit.
Research question:
What are the factors that contribute to timely and adequate oral intake in patients with HNC transitioning from tube
to oral feeding after undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
An observational single-centre exploratory prospective cohort study will be undertaken at UCLH which is a
centralised London service for four NHS-trusts, covering a wide-geographic area representative of people from
diverse, multiple ethnic backgrounds affected by HNC3,52,53.UCLH offers all discharge pathways for oral/tube
feeding which optimises generalisability, within the limitations of a single-centre design.
Data will be collected from electronic hospital records. All measures are routinely gathered except for the following
patient-recorded measures:
1-week diet diary once oral feeding commences.

A mixed-methods convergent-parallel approach over three years, across five workstreams:

2. Cross-sectional web-based survey of healthcare professionals working in UK HNC centres (n=65) to map

n exploring patient experiences
(n=10) and healthcare professionals (n=16-18) perceptions and practices of transitioning from tube-
feeding to optimal oral intake. Framework Analysis of transcripts and fieldnotes.
5. Development of a post-operative feeding toolkit with stakeholder consultation.
Impact
Patients:
Improved nutritional outcomes by identifying facilitators and barriers for achieving nutritional adequacy and optimal
timing for removing feeding tubes to avoid nutritional deficits. Improved return to swallowing function by identifying
optimal timing for re-commencing oral feeding.
Healthcare professionals:
Potential to change perioperative HNC feeding management by developing a toolkit that optimises decision-
making when transitioning patients from tube to oral intake, accounting for variation in local resources and
infrastructure.
NHS/service:
It is anticipated that streamlining oral feeding commencement and avoiding post-operative nutritional deficits will
reduce hospital stays, readmissions and associated costs.
Dissemination
Results will be disseminated in peer-reviewed journals targeted to the HNC readership and through conferences,
professional networks and websites

d investigating post-operative feeding practices and

compliment findings from the registered reviews.
Methods:
Scoping review ineligible for PROSPERO and therefore it will be registered with Open Science Framework or
Figshare.
Arksey and O’Malley (2005) 6-step framework47:

investigator independently against criteria:
Inclusion criteria:

This proposal aims to improve this pathway by filling evidence-gaps in post-operative feeding practices, to avoid
delayed and inadequate intake when transitioning from tube to oral feeding. This aligns with the 2019 James-Lind
Alliance Nutritional Screening and Malnutrition research priority ‘can nutritional-interventions lead to health
improvements’10.
Why is this research important?
This research falls under enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) initiatives. It should optimise post-operative
nutrition11with
feeding practices and patient, service, and economic benefits:
Patients benefits:
 Improved nutritional status by avoiding post-operative nutritional deficit; a modifiable risk factor for
malnutrition which is negatively associated with muscle-mass, immune response, wound healing, fatigue,
complications, morbidity/mortality, length-of-stay (LoS) and return-to-work12.
 Improved swallowing rehabilitation by reducing inactivity time which deconditions swallow
musculature13.This could be the difference between rehabilitating swallow or requiring life-long tube-
feeding8,14.
