Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I attended workshops through UCLH NIHR BRC from April to December 2022 on what PPI is and how to find/do
PPI. I was awarded a BRC PPI starter-grant (£300) in open competition (August 2022) for this research to form a
PPI group and run an initial group discussion.
I reached out to Barbara Reed, founder of Young Tongues, a support group for people with tongue cancer who
represent the majority affected by my research. Barbara disseminated my PPI advert and advised me to meet
face-to-face due to impact of surgery on speech intelligibility and understanding over a virtual/hybrid model. I
carefully selected five patients from my clinical caseload and Young Tongues, to ensure representation across
ethnicity (two Asian, three White-British), occupation, age (26-74years), gender (three women, two men),
disease/flap characteristics. Ethnicity was particularly important as my research is about eating/drinking post-
surgery, and cultural variation exists in diet.
PPI feedback included:

criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

criteria.pros: The application outlines a comprehensive plan to maximize public benefit, including co-production of manuscripts, attendance at patient conferences, and dissemination via social media, blogs, and animated videos tailored for patients. Strong links are made between study objectives (toolkit development) and anticipated benefits (improved care, reduced costs).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the dissemination and benefit plan is highly detailed and actionable.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the dissemination and benefit plan is highly detailed and actionable.

r70.
Methodology:
A convergence-coding matrix will be used to integrate findings from WS3 and WS4 to determine areas of
agreement, silence, and dissonance71(Figure 2). Desk-based development of toolkit will then be undertaken using
WS1-4 and convergence findings, MRC logic-modelling and PPI feedback/consultation. Stakeholder consultation
will then be conducted with six staff-representatives involved in decision-making (Consultant and Registrar
Surgeon, Dietitian, Speech-Therapist, ERAS-Nurse, Ward-Nurse) and 2 PPI-members. Questions from the 16-
item NPT toolkit will be used to frame the consultation and generate discussion to refine the toolkit which will be
amended accordingly using a series of iterations.
Analysis and outcome:
The toolkit will be evaluated in post-doctoral work.

To enter society, health/social care:

paper) published in open-access in peer-reviewed journals targeted to HNC/ERAS readership.

criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

criteria.pros: The proposal explicitly targets diverse demographics (ethnicity, age, disease stage) and acknowledges cultural variations in diet. It includes specific strategies to mitigate barriers, such as costed interpreters for patients with language barriers (14% of the population) and financial support for travel and childcare.
criteria.drawbacks: While strategies for language barriers are well-costed, specific mitigation strategies for other potential barriers (e.g., digital exclusion for online surveys, cognitive impairment in HNC patients) are less explicitly detailed, though general inclusivity is mentioned.
criteria.rationale: While strategies for language barriers are well-costed, specific mitigation strategies for other potential barriers (e.g., digital exclusion for online surveys, cognitive impairment in HNC patients) are less explicitly detailed, though general inclusivity is mentioned.

mplementable in different units,
and sites for a future multi-centre validation study.
WS3: Prospective cohort study [Months 10-29]
Rationale:
Limited empirical research has identified factors associated with timely/adequate nutritional intake after surgery. A
prospective cohort study will identify factors that may be predictive of nutritional adequacy that can be targeted in
the toolkit.
Research question:
What are the factors that contribute to timely and adequate oral intake in patients with HNC transitioning from tube
to oral feeding after undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
An observational single-centre exploratory prospective cohort study will be undertaken at UCLH which is a
centralised London service for four NHS-trusts, covering a wide-geographic area representative of people from
diverse, multiple ethnic backgrounds affected by HNC3,52,53.UCLH offers all discharge pathways for oral/tube
feeding which optimises generalisability, within the limitations of a single-centre design.
Data will be collected from electronic hospital records. All measures are routinely gathered except for the following
patient-recorded measures:
1-week diet diary once oral feeding commences.



estion:
What are the facilitators and barriers for transitioning from tube to adequate oral intake in patients with HNC after
undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
Rapid qualitative research techniques will be utilised to optimise efficiency by running data collection and analysis
in parallel. 1:1 semi-structured interviews will be conducted with patients and healthcare professionals and
unstructured observations of staff practices.
Sample:
Patient interviews:
A subset of patients participating in the cohort study will be invited off-treatment within 6-weeks of surgery.
Patients who have not recommenced oral intake in this timeframe will be excluded. Purposive sampling will be
used to ensure maximal variation in terms of sex, age, ethnicity, disease site/stage, type of flap and feeding tube.
General guidance indicates that 6-12 interviews is usually acceptable with little new information beyond 1560.
Recruitment will be until data saturation is achieved. Based on a similar study in colorectal cancer, a sample of 10
is anticipated61.
Staff interviews:
Healthcare professionals will be invited to participate using purposive sampling to represent different professional
roles and years

Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

criteria.drawbacks: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation of inclusion strategies during the project is implied through the iterative nature of the work but could be more explicitly stated as a formal monitoring mechanism.

5. Reporting: Results will be reported using PRISMA-Scr and mapped according to sources/types of

WS2: National Survey [Months 3-7]
Rationale:
Variation exists for timing to oral intake and processes for transitioning off tube-feeding5,9,14,31. This is associated
with local resources for placing gastrostomies and policies for discharging with naso-gastric tubes. A survey will
describe current reality and canvass opinions to understand the contexts the toolkit will be implemented.
Design:
A cross-sectional open web-based survey circulated within the UK.
Survey questi

ons will be developed using the review findings, alongside discussions between the research team
which includes key stakeholders (dietitian, speech-therapist, surgeon) and patients. Content validity will be
method48,by
established using Lawshe’s asking independent healthcare professionals to rate how essential each
question is to determine which to include/exclude.
An online, self-completed semi-structured questionnaire will be developed using Qualtrics software and piloted
with healthcare professionals reflective of the sample frame to ensure functionality. This will include multiple-option
answer, five-fold Likert scales and free-text comments asking about the centre demographic, timing to oral
feeding, tubes placed and removal processes.
Responses will be mandatory and options of ‘not applicable’ or ‘rather not say’ provided to ensure data-
completeness, with an opportunity to review/change answers before submitting.
Sample:
Multi-disciplinary leads at UK/NHS HNC surgical units (n=65) will be identified from the National HNC Audit
list49and approached via email with an explanation of the study and asked to disseminate within their units to staff

findings.implication: Applicant CV: Lists multiple research grants (NIHR, UCLH) and formal training (Biostatistics, MRes module), demonstrating relevant research experience and training.
Applicant CV: Extensive publication record (10+ papers) and awards (Best Abstract, Dean's List) confirm impactful research outputs and recognitions.
Applicant CV: Progression from Band 5 to Band 8a Clinical Lead and Associate Principal Investigator roles shows increasing responsibility and leadership trajectory.
Applicant CV: Current role as Senior Specialist Dietitian alongside research grants and publications demonstrates a clear practitioner-academic career path.
Applicant CV: High volume of peer-reviewed publications and successful grant applications indicate strong quality of previous research.
Applicant CV: Secured a PPI starter-grant to form a group and conduct discussions, showing prior integration of lived experience in research design.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly states a vision to improve nutritional care in HNC and a trajectory to become a clinical research leader, supporting g.1.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly identifies the fellowship as the 'crucial next step' to transition from developing academic to research leader, supporting g.2.
Applicant Research Background: Details specific research methods (SPSS, thematic analysis, audits, clinical trials) and formal training (MRes, MSc), strongly supporting g.3.
Applicant Research Background: Lists multiple publications in peer-reviewed journals, conference presentations, and awards (Best Abstract, Dean's List), confirming impactful outputs.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates increasing responsibility through Associate-PI roles, supervising dissertations, and leading clinical teams, supporting leadership trajectory.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly identifies as a 'developing clinical-academic' balancing full-time clinical work with research, strongly supporting g.6.
Applicant Research Background: Provides concrete examples of successful prior projects (service evaluations, audits, trials) and high-quality publications, supporting pr.8.
Applicant Research Background: Mentions securing a PPI starter-grant and incorporating patient feedback into proposal development, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly outlines the problem, aim, methods, and impact in accessible language, supporting pr.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Cites previous research statistics (40-72% patients) and identifies a gap in current practice regarding tube removal timing, supporting pr.2.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes a feasible multi-stage approach (review, survey, data collection) across 65 hospitals, supporting pr.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly details methods including literature review, staff surveys, and hospital record analysis, supporting pr.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Highlights direct patient benefits (recovery, wound healing) and NHS cost savings, supporting pr.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details patient involvement across all stages (design, data collection, review), supporting pr.6.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes specific co-design activities where patients checked information sheets and questions, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines dissemination plans via social media, blogs, and animated videos for patients, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Identifies knowledge gaps regarding optimal feeding practices and proposes a scoping review to map current literature, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines a feasible 3-year mixed-methods design with specific sample sizes (n=65, n=90) and five workstreams, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Details rigorous methods including scoping review, cross-sectional survey, prospective cohort with regression analysis, and qualitative framework analysis, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly lists impacts on patients (nutrition, swallowing), professionals (toolkit), and NHS (reduced stays/costs), supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Includes patient interviews, stakeholder consultation for toolkit development, and PPI advice for dissemination, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions stakeholder consultation for toolkit development and PPI advice for dissemination materials, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Describes dissemination via journals, conferences, social media, and animations tailored to patient audiences, supporting wp.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Critically reviews existing literature, identifying specific gaps such as selection bias, non-validated measures, and lack of data on protein intake, strongly supporting pr.2.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines a feasible 36-month project with 5 workstreams, specific sample sizes (n=90), and realistic recruitment targets, supporting pr.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Provides explicit methodological details including Arksey & O'Malley framework, PG-SGA outcomes, regression models, and sample size justification, supporting pr.4.
Detailed Research Plan: Quantifies patient benefits (reduced malnutrition, improved QoL) and economic impact (£90k-£13m savings), clearly supporting pr.5.
Detailed Research Plan: Integrates PPI in review consultation, survey development, and toolkit refinement, demonstrating strong engagement with people and communities, supporting pr.6.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly plans for diverse recruitment (ethnicity, age, disease stage) and includes costed interpreters to ensure inclusivity, supporting pr.9.
Detailed Research Plan: Identifies specific training needs (regression, rapid methods) and links them to project tasks and expert support, supporting td.1.
Detailed Research Plan: Maps training activities (statistician advice, rapid methods training) directly to identified skill gaps in analysis and qualitative work, supporting td.2.
Detailed Research Plan: Links the PhD and toolkit development to future post-doctoral funding and career trajectory as a research leader, supporting td.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Describes structured supervision (monthly meetings, thesis committee) and specific supervisor roles (surgical perspective, recruitment support), supporting ss.2.
Detailed Research Plan: Highlights UCLH as a centralised HNC centre with diverse patient populations and established networks, supporting ss.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Details PPI involvement in review consultation, survey design, and toolkit development, confirming integration of lived experience, supporting wp.1.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines comprehensive dissemination plans including patient animations, social media, and professional conferences, supporting wp.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly targets diverse demographics (ethnicity, age, disease stage) and plans for interpreters to mitigate barriers, supporting wp.4.
Detailed Research Plan: Ensures inclusivity across the lifecycle from PPI consultation in design to accessible dissemination formats, supporting wp.5.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly mentions 'Costed for interpreters' to reach all patients, providing a resourced plan for inclusion, supporting wp.6.

Patient & Public Involvement: Details specific design changes (e.g., outcome measures, interview questions, staff inclusion) based on PPI feedback, strongly supporting integration of lived experience.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicitly describes PPI group composition across ethnicity, age, gender, and occupation, demonstrating diversity in the research team.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines co-production of manuscripts, attendance at patient conferences, and dissemination strategies tailored to patient groups, supporting maximising public benefit.
Patient & Public Involvement: Highlights targeted recruitment of diverse ethnicities and ages, noting cultural variation in diet as a key consideration for representation.
Patient & Public Involvement: Confirms involvement across all stages from design to dissemination, including reviewing ethics applications and co-producing manuscripts.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicitly requests £11,712 to cover payment, subsistence, travel, and childcare for PPI activities, providing a costed plan for inclusion.

Training & Development and Research Support: Clearly articulates a vision to become a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading a research group, with a detailed training plan supporting this trajectory.

Training & Development and Research Support: Identifies specific skill gaps (e.g., PPI facilitation, scoping reviews, qualitative methods) and maps targeted training to bridge them, justifying the award as the necessary

Training & Development and Research Support: Details prior experience in quantitative research and statistics, while outlining a robust plan to acquire missing skills in qualitative methods and complex intervention desig

Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly states career ambitions to lead an ERAS Centre of Excellence and includes leadership training (Rosalind Franklin Programme) and mentorship to support this trajector

Training & Development and Research Support: Demonstrates commitment to a practitioner-academic career through planned clinical practice (1 day/fortnight), clinical governance meetings, and maintaining specialist dietiti

Training & Development and Research Support: Provides a comprehensive and clear training description covering quantitative, qualitative, and implementation science methods, directly relevant to the proposed mixed-methods

Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly identifies personal skill gaps (e.g., PPI facilitation, ethnography, NVivo) and maps specific courses and workshops to address these needs for the project.

Training & Development and Research Support: Connects training activities (ERAS courses, leadership academy) directly to long-term career goals of becoming a clinical academic leader and establishing an ERAS Centre.

Training & Development and Research Support: Highlights the strong track record of supervisors (Moonesinghe, Govender, Vindrola-Padros) with extensive publications, leadership roles, and successful PhD supervision.

Training & Development and Research Support: Describes a robust supervisory framework with monthly meetings, a thesis committee, and specific expertise alignment (e.g., rapid methods, clinical dietetics) for the project.

Training & Development and Research Support: UCLH and UCL provide extensive infrastructure, including the RDTI department, BRC funding, and specific support frameworks (CNMAR) aligned with the applicant's career aspirati

Training & Development and Research Support: UCLH and UCL demonstrate strong commitment to inclusivity through Athena Swan awards, BAME/LGBTQ+ networks, and explicit strategies to support underrepresented groups in resea

Training & Development and Research Support: Outlines extensive collaboration with patient groups (Young Tongues), professional bodies (BAHNO, BDA), and industry (Nutricia) to ensure dissemination and implementation of f
SUMMARY BUDGET: Details training costs (tuition, short courses) and NHS support costs (eligibility, consent) with clear links to project phases and regulatory requirements, supporting pr.7.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Costs for patient interview support (vouchers, travel, childcare, interpreters) are explicitly detailed, ensuring barriers to participation are financially addressed, supporting wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides financial support for patient participants (vouchers, travel, childcare, interpreters) to mitigate barriers to participation, supporting pr.9.

Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show high readability (Flesch-Kincaid 6.34, 65.47 ease), low jargon density (0.98%), and coverage of key elements (problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, impact), strongly su
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Plain English Summary and NLP analysis sections, indicating structural compliance for readability and content checks.
Application Form Analysis: Identifies a detailed SUMMARY BUDGET section with 415 table-like lines, confirming the presence of costed resource justification.
